title: Switzerland Talks Conclude First Round, Colombia Elects Outsider, Iran's Hardliners Push Back
subhead: US-Iran negotiators wrap an 80-minute session in Switzerland and agree on a Lebanon de-confliction cell, but Iran claims to have shut the Strait of Hormuz again and supreme leader Khamenei's inner circle is fractured. Colombia's election delivers a Trump-backed outsider by the slimmest of margins. Bolivia's state of emergency lifts blockades. India's Cockroach movement camps out in Delhi.

sections.heading: Switzerland Talks End Round One With a De-Confliction Cell — Not a Breakthrough
sections.body:
US Vice President JD Vance and Iran's lead negotiators — parliamentary speaker Mohammad Bagher Qalibaf and foreign minister Abbas Araghchi — held roughly 80 minutes of direct talks in Obbuergen, Switzerland, on Sunday, mediated by Pakistan and Qatar. The session concluded early Monday with a joint statement announcing a 'de-confliction cell' to address fighting in Lebanon, including the Lebanese government, tasked with ensuring the 'adherence of the termination of military operations' on that front. Lower-level technical talks will continue through the week, with the aim of preparing groundwork for senior officials to return and sign agreements later.

A senior US diplomat, speaking on condition of anonymity, said the session included clarifying Iran's statements about the Strait of Hormuz and discussing 'mechanisms' to keep the waterway open. Nuclear enrichment was also discussed — Iran's president Masoud Pezeshkian declared that 'we will never back down from the right to enrich uranium,' while Trump, in a Fox News interview, warned Pezeshkian to 'watch what he says' and threatened to take over Iran. The MoU's 60-day clock for a permanent deal is now running.

Iran, meanwhile, maintained that it had again closed the Strait of Hormuz over the weekend, citing Israel's continued strikes in Lebanon. The US military said shipping traffic continued. Neither Israel nor Hezbollah is a signatory to the US-Iran deal, and Netanyahu has vowed to keep Israeli forces in southern Lebanon until all threats are eliminated.
sections.whyItMatters: The de-confliction cell is a procedural step forward but falls far short of the ceasefire enforcement Iran demands. The 60-day timeline now ticks against two open questions: whether Israel will constrain its Lebanon operations, and whether Iran will keep Hormuz open long enough for technical talks to produce a lasting deal.

sections.heading: Khamenei's Hardline Opposition Mobilizes Against the Deal
sections.body:
While diplomats met in Switzerland, Tehran's internal divisions burst into the open. Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei issued a rare written statement saying he 'held a different view' on the MoU but approved it after President Pezeshkian accepted responsibility — his only public comment since becoming supreme leader in March. Hardline cleric and legislator Mahmoud Nabavian then went on state television's IRINN channel and read what he claimed was correspondence between Khamenei and the Supreme National Security Council, asserting the supreme leader opposed the April 8 ceasefire and wanted Iran to impose a tolling system on Strait of Hormuz shipping and demand 'exclusive management' of the waterway. The interview ran for 11 minutes before being abruptly cut; IRINN's director general resigned the next day.

Ultra-hardliner Saeed Jalili is the only known member of the Supreme National Security Council to have voted against the deal, according to multiple officials cited by Al Jazeera. Hardline mayor Alireza Zakani and state-linked media are pushing back against the agreement, with one wartime TV programme calling for Tehran's Mehrabad Airport to be closed to prevent negotiators from leaving the country. Tasnim news agency editor Kian Abdollahi called it a 'mistake' to send a delegation to Switzerland while the MoU is not being enforced.
sections.whyItMatters: Iran's domestic opposition to the deal is not just rhetorical — it has access to state media, intelligence on the supreme leader's private deliberations, and the ability to force resignations at state broadcasters. The hardliners' strategy is to frame the MoU as capitulation, and the de-confliction cell's success or failure in Lebanon will directly fuel or undermine that campaign.

sections.heading: Colombia Elects Trump-Backed Outsider by a Hair
sections.body:
Political newcomer Abelardo de la Espriella, a 47-year-old business owner and lawyer with dual Colombian and US citizenship, held a razor-thin lead in Colombia's presidential runoff with 49.7% of the vote to progressive rival Ivan Cepeda's 48.7%, with 99.9% of results counted. Election officials have not formally declared a winner. Cepeda announced his campaign would challenge results from more than 30,000 voting stations, and outgoing president Gustavo Petro also vowed to contest the outcome. No recount has ever flipped a presidential result in Colombian history.

De la Espriella, nicknamed 'The Tiger,' campaigned on an iron-fist approach to crime and drug trafficking, promising to end Petro's failed peace negotiations with armed groups and build mega-prisons modeled on El Salvador's Bukele model. He earned Trump's endorsement and is a registered Republican. US Secretary of State Marco Rubio and Argentina's president Milei were among the first to congratulate him, while Trump posted 'He Won, BIG!' on his social media platform. The election took place against a backdrop of 14,780 homicides last year — the most since at least 2015 — and the assassination of conservative presidential candidate Miguel Uribe. Colombia's illegal armed groups have more than 27,000 members.
sections.whyItMatters: A one-point margin means nearly half the country rejected the hardline approach, and Cepeda's challenge to 30,000 stations could drag out the result. De la Espriella's victory is effectively a repudiation of Petro's peace-process agenda, but his Bukele-style promises will be tested quickly against Colombia's deeply entrenched armed groups and the reality that the worst violence is in the periphery, not the core regions where his message resonated most.

sections.heading: Bolivia Lifts Blockades Under Emergency Powers After 17 Deaths
sections.body:
Bolivia's Legislative Assembly approved president Rodrigo Paz's state of emergency decree on Sunday, prohibiting the blocking of roads and authorizing the military to support police in restoring order. The decree came after five weeks of anti-government protests that stranded trucks and choked supplies of food, fuel, and medicines across the country. The national highway authority reported no active blockades on Sunday for the first time in weeks, though many roads still require significant repair.

At least 17 people have died, many from disruptions to medical care caused by the blockades, according to Bolivia's ombudsman's office. Some 365 arrests and 37 injuries have been reported. In Santa Cruz, officials and protest leaders signed an agreement to lift a critical blockade at San Julian. A federation of rural and Indigenous groups paused protests in La Paz while maintaining their demands. The protests were triggered by Paz's austerity measures, including cuts to fuel subsidies. Human rights organizations warned that a harsh government response that fails to address underlying grievances could compound unrest in the long term.
sections.whyItMatters: Bolivia's emergency decree has cleared the roads for now, but the underlying dispute over fuel subsidies and austerity remains unresolved. The pause by protest groups is tactical, not ideological — and if the government does not address the economic grievances, the blockades are likely to return.

sections.heading: India's Cockroach Movement Digs In as Exam Crisis Deepens
sections.body:
Supporters of the Cockroach Janta Party, a Gen Z political movement that began as a social media joke, spent a second night camped at Jantar Mantar in New Delhi, demanding the resignation of federal education minister Dharmendra Pradhan. The movement, led by recent Boston University graduate Abhijeet Dipke, was sparked by the chief justice's comment equating young people with cockroaches and has since channelled anger over frequent exam paper leaks and scoring irregularities. Its Instagram following has surpassed 22 million — double that of India's ruling BJP.

Nearly 1.7 million students retook India's top medical entrance exam on Sunday after the initial exam was cancelled over a leaked question paper. In the days between the two exam dates, more than a dozen students across India died by suicide. The government temporarily banned Telegram to curb leaks, a move critics called a Band-Aid solution. Delhi police briefly cut off water and food access to the protesters. Dipke told Al Jazeera: 'If the government thinks they can exhaust us, they are mistaken. We will remain here.'
sections.whyItMatters: With nearly half of India's 1.4 billion people under 25, the Cockroach movement is the first sustained, youth-driven political challenge to Modi's 12-year rule that does not revolve around communal or religious identity. If it sustains momentum, it could force the first ministerial resignation of this government — and if it doesn't, the underlying despair over exam integrity and youth unemployment will only deepen.

sections.heading: Gaza Ceasefire Under Strain as Journalist Killed, Lebanon Front Persists
sections.body:
Israeli strikes in Gaza killed at least six people on Saturday, including Al Jazeera cameraman Ahmed Wishah, who was killed in a strike on Bureij refugee camp. The IDF accused Wishah of being 'a terrorist in Hamas' military wing who served as a sniper operative' but provided no evidence. Wishah's brother Mohamed, also an Al Jazeera correspondent, was killed in an Israeli strike in April. In a separate strike in Gaza City's Sabra neighbourhood, four family members including two children were killed overnight, according to the civil defence agency and a local hospital.

Gaza's Hamas-run health ministry says 1,007 Palestinians have been killed by Israeli forces since the October ceasefire took effect. The UN's humanitarian chief Tom Fletcher told the Security Council that hunger in Gaza had dropped from 92% of households to 36% since more aid trucks began entering, but 70% of the population still lacks proper shelter and essential services remain 'on the brink.' Netanyahu has directed the IDF to expand Israeli-controlled territory in Gaza to 70% of the strip, despite the ceasefire agreement stipulating a progressive Israeli withdrawal and no reoccupation.
sections.whyItMatters: The Gaza ceasefire's core contradiction remains: the agreement says Israel will progressively withdraw and not occupy Gaza, while Netanyahu has publicly ordered expansion to 70% control. Each Israeli strike that kills civilians under a nominal ceasefire erodes what little trust remains — and the Lebanon front's instability gives Iran justification to escalate on Hormuz, linking all three fronts in a single chain of pressure.

bottomLine: The Switzerland talks produced a de-confliction cell for Lebanon — a mechanism, not a resolution. Iran's hardliners are openly sabotaging the deal from inside Tehran, while the supreme leader himself disavowed it in writing. Colombia elected a Trump-backed political newcomer by roughly one point, a result that will be contested. Bolivia's blockade crisis paused under emergency powers but the fuel subsidy dispute remains. India's Gen Z Cockroach movement is the first youth-driven political headache Modi has faced in 12 years. And in Gaza and Lebanon, ceasefires continue to be more word than reality — which is precisely the condition Iran uses to justify keeping the Strait of Hormuz in play.
